faq: Why is an interface showing operationally down while administratively up?
likely_causes:
- Physical cable disconnected or pulled
- Optical SFP/QSFP module failed or absent
- Loss of Signal (LOS) on fiber
- Remote end shutdown or failed
- Patch panel or cross-connect break
observability_signals:
- oper_status == down, admin_status == up
- DDM/DOM optical Rx power below threshold
- LOS/LOF alarms on interface
- SNMP ifOperStatus trap
- Syslog: 'line protocol is down'
cli_commands:
show interface <int>
show interface <int> transceiver
show logging | inc down|LOS
ddm interface <int>
show inventory | inc <int>
rca_steps:
1) Confirm admin_status=up, oper_status=down — rules out admin shutdown
2) Check DDM optical Rx power — below -20 dBm indicates fiber/SFP fault
3) Inspect cable and patch panel connection physically
4) Swap SFP transceiver with known-good unit
5) Test with alternate fiber strand or patch cable
6) Check remote end interface for reciprocal down state
resolution: Replace faulty SFP/cable; reconnect physical cable; restore remote end interface; repair fiber strand.
preventive_action: Use certified optics; maintain spare SFPs; enable DDM threshold alerts; deploy UDLD for fiber pair detection.
related_tools: DDM/DOM, SNMP traps, Syslog, OTDR
priority: P1 if uplink or core link; P2 for access

faq: Why does an interface keep cycling up and down (flapping)?
likely_causes:
- Loose or damaged patch cable causing intermittent contact
- Auto-negotiation failing due to NIC or switch incompatibility
- Speed/duplex mismatch between peers
- Faulty SFP with marginal optical power
- PoE brownout causing device reboot loop
- Remote device restarting or rebooting
observability_signals:
- flap_count > 3 in monitoring window
- oper_changes_per_min > 1
- Syslog repeated 'changed state to up/down'
- Interface error counters incrementing each flap cycle
- SNMP linkUp/linkDown trap storms
cli_commands:
show interface <int> | inc flap|change
show logging | inc 'changed state'
show interface <int> counters
show power inline <int>
show interface <int> transceiver
rca_steps:
1) Count flap events and note interval pattern — periodic = likely power/PoE; random = cable/SFP
2) Check syslog timestamp correlation with power events
3) Inspect and reseat cable at both ends
4) Test with known-good cable
5) Check auto-negotiation: lock speed/duplex manually and observe
6) If PoE device: check PoE budget and power draw per port
resolution: Replace cable; lock speed/duplex; replace marginal SFP; adjust PoE allocation; update NIC firmware/driver.
preventive_action: Enable carrier-delay timer to dampen flaps; set link debounce; use err-disable recovery with dampening.
related_tools: Syslog, SNMP traps, PoE monitoring
priority: P2/P3; P1 if uplink flapping

faq: CRC and input errors are increasing on an interface — what is causing this?
likely_causes:
- Defective patch cable or SFP transceiver
- Duplex mismatch causing late collisions interpreted as CRC
- EMI on unshielded copper cable
- Marginal optical signal below Rx sensitivity
- Egress queue overflow causing output discards
- Oversubscribed uplink causing tail-drop
observability_signals:
- in_errors > 100 per polling interval
- out_discards > 100
- CRC counter incrementing (show interface)
- DDM Rx power below threshold
- Late collisions > 0
cli_commands:
show interface <int> counters errors
show interface <int> | inc CRC|error|discard
ddm interface <int>
show platform hardware qfp drops
show policy-map interface <int>
rca_steps:
1) Identify error type: CRC (physical), discards (congestion), late collisions (duplex)
2) CRC errors: check DDM optical level; swap cable and SFP
3) Late collisions: check duplex config on both ends
4) Output discards: check interface utilization and QoS queue drops
5) Correlate error timestamps with traffic bursts (NetFlow)
6) Test on alternate port/path to isolate hardware vs path
resolution: Replace cable/SFP for CRC; fix duplex mismatch; tune QoS or add bandwidth for discards.
preventive_action: Set SNMP threshold alerts on error counters; use error-baseline alerts; deploy certified optics.
related_tools: SNMP, NetFlow, DDM, Wireshark
priority: P2; P1 if errors causing significant packet loss

faq: Interface shows high Rx errors but zero Tx errors — is this a unidirectional link?
likely_causes:
- One fiber strand in a pair broken or mispatched (Tx/Rx crossed)
- Dirty or damaged fiber connector on receive strand only
- Remote Tx SFP failed while local Tx is healthy
- Fiber splice degraded on receive path
- UDLD not configured to detect and shutdown the unidirectional port
observability_signals:
- rx_errors > 100, tx_errors == 0
- DDM Rx power critically low; Tx power normal
- UDLD neighbor missing or unidirectional state
- Remote device shows opposite error pattern
- STP may be seeing topology changes due to one-way BPDUs
cli_commands:
show interface <int> | inc rx|tx|error
show interface <int> transceiver
show udld <int>
show udld neighbors
ping <remote_ip> — check if response returns
rca_steps:
1) Confirm asymmetric error pattern: high rx_errors, zero tx_errors
2) Check DDM Rx power on local interface — critically low confirms fiber strand issue
3) Check remote device DDM Tx power — normal confirms remote Tx is healthy, local Rx fiber broken
4) Inspect and clean fiber connector on Rx strand
5) Test with alternate fiber patch; OTDR test on suspect strand
6) Enable UDLD aggressive mode to auto-detect and shutdown unidirectional ports
resolution: Replace broken fiber strand; clean Rx connector; enable UDLD; correct any Tx/Rx crossover in patch panel.
preventive_action: Deploy UDLD globally on all fiber links; monitor DDM Rx power per interface; use OTDR after any fiber work.
related_tools: UDLD, DDM, OTDR, Syslog
priority: P1 — STP loops or routing failures may result

faq: Interface shows late collisions and performance issues — could this be a duplex mismatch?
likely_causes:
- Auto-negotiation failure between switch and NIC
- One end hard-coded to full-duplex; other in half-duplex after autoneg
- Older NIC not supporting 1G autoneg correctly
- Speed mismatch: one end 100M, other 1G
- Third-party SFP incompatibility causing negotiation failure
observability_signals:
- collisions > 10 on interface
- speed_mismatch_flag == 1
- Late collisions incrementing (not early)
- CRC errors on one side, no errors on other
- Interface throughput far below rated capacity
cli_commands:
show interface <int> | inc duplex|speed|collision
show run interface <int> | inc duplex|speed
show interface <int> capabilities
show cdp neighbors <int> detail | inc duplex|Platform
rca_steps:
1) Check duplex config: 'show interface' — look for half/full mismatch
2) Compare both ends: CDP/LLDP shows peer duplex setting
3) Look for late collisions (not early) — definitive duplex mismatch indicator
4) Set both ends to explicit speed and duplex (no autoneg) temporarily
5) If NIC-related: update NIC driver; force to full-duplex in OS NIC settings
6) Replace SFP if third-party autoneg incompatibility suspected
resolution: Explicitly set speed and duplex on both ends; update NIC driver; replace incompatible SFP with vendor-certified module.
preventive_action: Standardize on autoneg for all copper Ethernet; explicitly set for known problem NIC models; use CDP/LLDP to audit duplex mismatches.
related_tools: CDP, LLDP, SNMP, Syslog
priority: P2 — performance degradation; P1 if causing outage

faq: Interface utilization is above 90% and queue drops are occurring — how to diagnose and resolve?
likely_causes:
- Traffic volume exceeding interface capacity
- Backup or bulk transfer jobs flooding the link
- QoS not prioritizing critical traffic — all in same queue
- Incorrect shaping rate set below actual link capacity
- Microbursts exceeding buffer depth causing tail-drop
- Lack of WRED causing synchronized TCP backoff
observability_signals:
- utilization_percent > 90
- out_discards > 0
- Queue depth at or near maximum
- WRED drop statistics incrementing
- NetFlow showing bulk/backup traffic consuming bandwidth
cli_commands:
show interface <int> | inc rate|utilization
show policy-map interface <int>
show queue <int>
ip nbar protocol-discovery interface <int>
show ip flow top-talkers
rca_steps:
1) Identify top talker via NetFlow — is it backup, rsync, or unexpected flow?
2) Check QoS policy: is critical traffic in LLQ/priority queue?
3) Inspect queue depth and WRED discard statistics per class
4) Verify interface shaping rate matches provisioned CIR
5) Determine if issue is sustained (capacity) vs burst (buffering)
6) For sustained: plan bandwidth upgrade or traffic engineering
resolution: Implement QoS classification and priority queuing; rate-limit bulk flows; upgrade link capacity; add WRED for TCP fairness.
preventive_action: Set utilization alerts at 70%/85%; deploy QoS before congestion occurs; schedule backups during off-peak hours.
related_tools: NetFlow, NBAR, SNMP, IPSLA
priority: P1 if voice/critical apps affected; P2 otherwise

faq: High jitter is impacting VoIP/RTP quality — what are the causes and remediation steps?
likely_causes:
- Microbursts on shared WAN link causing transient queue delay
- Missing or incorrect DSCP marking on RTP traffic
- QoS LLQ bandwidth insufficient for voice load
- WAN shaping Tc interval too long causing burst delay
- Serialization delay on low-speed links
- ISP upstream jitter on last-mile
observability_signals:
- jitter_ms > 30 on IPSLA probes
- packet_loss_percent > 1 on voice path
- MOS score below 3.5
- Voice queue showing drops
- DSCP EF traffic not in priority queue
cli_commands:
show ip sla statistics
show policy-map interface <wan-int>
show ip sla summary
iperf3 -u -b 1M --dscp 46
show interface <int> | inc rate
rca_steps:
1) Confirm jitter via IPSLA MOS probe — baseline vs current
2) Check DSCP marking of RTP: 'show policy-map interface' — is EF in LLQ?
3) Inspect LLQ bandwidth allocation vs active voice calls
4) Check WAN utilization for microbursts using 30-sec polling intervals
5) Test path with iperf3 UDP to measure jitter independently
6) If WAN: check shaping Tc interval; reduce to 10ms for voice
resolution: Enforce DSCP EF marking at voice edge; increase LLQ bandwidth; reduce shaping Tc interval; engage ISP for last-mile SLA.
preventive_action: Deploy IPSLA MOS probes for continuous voice quality monitoring; set jitter alert at 20ms; validate QoS post-change.
related_tools: IPSLA/TWAMP, NetFlow, Wireshark, iperf3
priority: P1 for active voice service degradation

faq: BGP session to a neighbor has gone down — what is the triage and recovery process?
likely_causes:
- Physical link failure on BGP peering interface
- TCP session dropped due to MTU/MSS issue
- Hold timer expired — CPU overload delayed keepalives
- MD5 authentication key mismatch
- Max-prefix limit reached causing notification and teardown
- Remote peer restarted or reconfigured
- ACL blocking BGP TCP port 179
observability_signals:
- bgp_session_state == down
- Syslog: BGP-5-ADJCHANGE neighbor down
- BGP notification message type in logs
- Prefix count near max-prefix threshold before teardown
- Interface flap coinciding with BGP down event
cli_commands:
show bgp summary
show ip bgp neighbors <peer>
show logging | inc BGP|ADJCHANGE
show interface <peer-int> counters
show access-list | inc 179
ping <peer-ip> source <local-ip>
rca_steps:
1) Check BGP notification type in syslog — HOLD_TIMER, CEASE, AUTH, MAX_PREFIX
2) Verify TCP 179 reachability: 'telnet <peer> 179'
3) Check interface errors for physical-layer cause
4) Validate MD5 key matches on both sides
5) Check prefix count vs max-prefix threshold
6) Confirm CPU not overloaded — delayed keepalives cause hold timer expiry
7) Check MTU: BGP OPEN packets must not be fragmented
resolution: Restore physical link; fix MD5 key; raise or reset max-prefix; reduce CPU load; fix MTU/MSS; open TCP 179 in ACL.
preventive_action: Set BFD for fast BGP failure detection; configure max-prefix with restart-timer; alert on BGP adj-change syslog.
related_tools: BGP logs, Syslog, BFD, SNMP, Packet capture
priority: P1 — loss of BGP routes causes traffic black-holing

faq: OSPF adjacency is repeatedly dropping and re-forming — what should I investigate?
likely_causes:
- Hello packets being dropped due to link congestion or loss
- CPU overload causing OSPF process to miss hello transmission
- Dead timer expired — hello interval too long vs dead timer
- MTU mismatch causing DBD exchange failure after each reset
- Authentication key rotation causing brief mismatch
- BFD aggressiveness triggering spurious OSPF reset
observability_signals:
- ospf_adj_changes > 3 in monitoring window
- Syslog: LOST_ADJACENCY, Dead timer expired
- Route instability (prefix flapping in routing table)
- OSPF SPF re-runs increasing
- CPU spikes coinciding with adjacency drops
cli_commands:
show ip ospf neighbor
show ip ospf statistics
show logging | inc OSPF|ADJCHANGE|Dead
show processes cpu | inc OSPF
show ip ospf interface <int>
rca_steps:
1) Check syslog for LOST_ADJACENCY reason — dead timer vs auth vs MTU
2) Review OSPF hello/dead timers: must match on both ends
3) Correlate adj drops with CPU spikes — OSPF starved?
4) Check interface error rates at time of flap — packet loss causing hello miss
5) If BFD associated: check BFD timers vs link quality
6) Verify MTU matches after each reset (EXSTART→DOWN cycles = MTU issue)
resolution: Align hello/dead timers; reduce OSPF SPF throttle; fix CPU overload; resolve link loss; set 'ip ospf mtu-ignore' as workaround for MTU.
preventive_action: Set OSPF logging adjacency changes; monitor adj-change count via SNMP; use OSPF graceful restart (NSF).
related_tools: OSPF logs, Syslog, SNMP, BFD
priority: P1 if causing route instability and traffic drops; P2 if isolated

faq: Large packets are being fragmented or dropped on the WAN path — how to diagnose MTU mismatch?
likely_causes:
- WAN link MTU smaller than LAN MTU (e.g., PPPoE overhead reduces to 1492)
- DF-bit set on large packets causing ICMP 'fragmentation needed' to be generated
- ICMP type 3 code 4 being blocked by firewall — PMTUD black-hole
- IPsec or GRE tunnel overhead reducing effective MTU
- Provider enforcing lower MTU without notifying customer
- Jumbo frames enabled on LAN side but not on WAN
observability_signals:
- fragmented_packets > 100
- icmp_frag_needed > 0 at WAN interface
- Large transfers fail; small transfers succeed (classic PMTUD black-hole)
- Ping with DF-bit and large size fails at specific size
- TCP sessions establish but hang or terminate unexpectedly
cli_commands:
ping <dst> df-bit size 1472
ping <dst> df-bit size 1400
show interface <wan-int> | inc MTU
show ip tcp adjust-mss
traceroute <dst> (check for ICMP unreachable hops)
show ip traffic | inc frag
rca_steps:
1) Test with 'ping df-bit size 1472' — if fails, find max working size by binary search
2) Identify which hop drops the large packet via traceroute MTU discovery
3) Check if ICMP type 3 code 4 is being blocked (firewall rule)
4) Calculate correct MTU: WAN MTU minus tunnel overhead (GRE=24B, IPsec=50-70B)
5) Apply TCP MSS clamping: 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1452' on WAN interface
6) Set interface MTU to match WAN provider-enforced MTU
resolution: Set correct MTU on WAN interface; apply TCP MSS clamping; allow ICMP type 3 code 4 through firewall; set tunnel interface MTU.
preventive_action: Test PMTUD with DF-bit ping after any WAN change; deploy MSS clamping as standard on all WAN interfaces.
related_tools: Ping (DF-bit), Traceroute, Wireshark, SNMP
priority: P2 for partial failures; P1 if all TCP sessions affected

faq: Multiple wireless clients failing to authenticate to the SSID — how to diagnose?
likely_causes:
- RADIUS server unreachable from WLC/AP
- EAP supplicant misconfiguration on client
- Certificate expired on RADIUS or client
- Shared secret mismatch between WLC and RADIUS
- RADIUS server overloaded or out of licenses
- 802.1X VLAN assignment attribute missing or incorrect
- Client NIC driver issue with EAP method
observability_signals:
- wifi_auth_failures > 20 on SSID
- RADIUS Access-Reject rate increasing
- EAP failure messages in WLC logs
- RADIUS server not responding from WLC perspective
- Specific EAP type failures (PEAP, EAP-TLS)
cli_commands:
show wireless client summary
show aaa servers
show radius statistics
show logging | inc EAP|RADIUS|auth
test aaa group <radius-group> <user> <pw>
ping <radius-ip> source <wlc-mgmt-ip>
rca_steps:
1) Check RADIUS reachability: ping from WLC management IP to RADIUS server IP
2) Review RADIUS statistics: Access-Request vs Accept vs Reject ratio
3) Test AAA authentication: 'test aaa group' command
4) Inspect WLC logs for specific EAP failure type
5) Check RADIUS server logs for reject reason (wrong password, expired cert, no matching policy)
6) Verify shared secret matches on WLC and RADIUS
7) Check certificate expiry if EAP-TLS in use
resolution: Restore RADIUS reachability; fix shared secret; renew expired certificate; correct RADIUS policy; add MAB fallback.
preventive_action: Deploy redundant RADIUS; monitor auth success rate; set cert expiry alerts 60 days out; test auth monthly.
related_tools: WLC logs, RADIUS logs, Syslog, Packet capture
priority: P1 if all users unable to connect; P2 for subset

faq: Device CPU is sustained above 80% — what are the causes and how to remediate?
likely_causes:
- Runaway process or software bug consuming CPU
- Control plane traffic flood (DDoS, misconfigured keep-alive)
- Excessive SNMP polling rate
- Routing protocol instability causing constant SPF/DUAL recalculation
- Antivirus or security scan consuming CPU
- Insufficient hardware for current load
observability_signals:
- cpu_percent > 80 sustained for > 5 minutes
- Top process consuming > 50% CPU
- SNMP response latency increasing
- CoPP drops indicating control-plane overload
- System responsiveness degraded (SSH slow)
cli_commands:
show processes cpu sorted
show processes cpu history
show control-plane statistics
show logging | inc CPU|high
top (Linux host)
ps aux --sort=-%cpu (Linux host)
rca_steps:
1) Identify top CPU-consuming process — is it expected?
2) Check process trend: sudden spike or gradual growth?
3) For network device: check CoPP drops — control-plane attack?
4) For server: correlate with scheduled jobs, scans, or deployments
5) Check routing protocol stability — OSPF/BGP reconvergence consuming CPU
6) Review SNMP poll frequency — reduce if excessive
resolution: Kill/restart runaway process; enable CoPP; reduce SNMP poll rate; upgrade hardware; fix routing instability.
preventive_action: Set CPU alert at 70%; enable CoPP on all network devices; stagger SNMP polling; monitor process CPU trend.
related_tools: SNMP, Syslog, top/ps, CoPP stats
priority: P1 if control plane affected or device unresponsive

faq: Host shows short-duration CPU spikes — how to identify and manage transient CPU bursts?
likely_causes:
- Scheduled cron job or batch task running at peak
- Backup agent starting a job
- Log rotation or database maintenance task
- Garbage collection in JVM/runtime
- Compilation or software update running
- Sudden burst of user requests hitting the host
observability_signals:
- cpu_percent > 90 for < 2 minutes, then recovering
- cpu_load_1min > 4 during spike
- Correlation with cron schedule time
- Application latency spike coinciding with CPU burst
- No sustained high CPU between spikes
cli_commands:
top -b -n 1 (Linux)
ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head -10
crontab -l; cat /etc/cron.d/*
sar -u 1 60 (Linux sar)
jstat -gcutil <pid> (JVM GC check)
show processes cpu history (network device)
rca_steps:
1) Capture process list during spike: 'top -b' or 'ps aux'
2) Correlate spike timestamp with cron schedule
3) Check for JVM GC: frequent full GC causes CPU spikes
4) Review backup agent logs for job start times
5) If user-driven: analyze request rate at spike time
6) Determine if spike causes application SLA breach
resolution: Reschedule batch jobs to off-peak; tune JVM GC settings; increase instance size; implement autoscaling for burst.
preventive_action: Stagger scheduled jobs; monitor with 1-min CPU polling; set spike alert with minimum duration (avoid noise).
related_tools: sar, top, cron logs, APM, JVM metrics
priority: P3 if self-recovering; P2 if impacting SLA

faq: Host memory utilization is above 80% — what to investigate?
likely_causes:
- Too many processes running in parallel exceeding RAM
- Application not releasing memory (early-stage leak)
- OS page cache consuming memory (often benign)
- Large dataset loaded into memory by application
- Container/VM not resource-bounded
- Memory over-provisioning on hypervisor
observability_signals:
- mem_percent > 80 sustained
- Swap utilization increasing
- OOM killer events in kernel logs
- Application latency increasing with memory pressure
- High page-fault rate
cli_commands:
free -m (Linux)
cat /proc/meminfo
ps aux --sort=-%mem | head -10
vmstat 1 10
kill -0 <pid> (check OOM events)
journalctl -k | grep -i 'oom\|killed'
rca_steps:
1) Check if OS page cache is inflating memory — 'free -m' available column
2) Identify top memory consumers: 'ps aux --sort=-%mem'
3) Check swap usage and page-in/out rate (vmstat)
4) Look for OOM killer events in kernel log
5) Check if memory is growing over time (leak) or stable at high level
6) Correlate with application load — is this expected for current user count?
resolution: Increase RAM; reduce concurrent processes; tune application memory limits; expand swap as temporary measure; restart leaking process.
preventive_action: Set OOM kill alerts; monitor swap utilization; resource-bound containers; capacity plan based on growth trend.
related_tools: vmstat, free, journalctl, SNMP
priority: P2; P1 if OOM killing critical processes

faq: Memory usage is steadily growing over time — how to confirm and address a memory leak?
likely_causes:
- Application not freeing heap memory after use
- Native library (C/C++) leaking without JVM visibility
- Connection pool or thread pool growing without bounds
- Cache growing without eviction policy
- File descriptors or sockets not closed properly
- Third-party library bug
observability_signals:
- mem_percent > 85 and growing
- mem_trend_1h > 10% per hour
- Process RSS memory growing monotonically
- OutOfMemoryError in application logs
- Process requires periodic restart to recover memory
cli_commands:
ps -o pid,rss,vsz,comm -p <pid>
cat /proc/<pid>/status | grep VmRSS
valgrind --leak-check=full <binary> (C/C++)
jmap -heap <pid> (JVM)
jstack <pid> (JVM thread dump)
pmap -d <pid> (memory map)
rca_steps:
1) Confirm leak: plot RSS memory over 24h — linear growth = leak
2) Compare mem_trend_1h with load — growing even when load is flat = leak
3) For JVM: use 'jmap -heap' and heap dump for analysis
4) For native: use Valgrind or AddressSanitizer
5) Correlate leak rate with specific code paths (connection open, request handling)
6) Identify last code change before leak started
resolution: Apply code fix or vendor patch; implement periodic restart as emergency; add heap dump trigger on OOM; rollback to previous version.
preventive_action: Implement heap trend monitoring; set RSS growth rate alert; mandatory heap profiling in load testing.
related_tools: jmap, Valgrind, pmap, APM heap profiler
priority: P2 — becomes P1 as memory exhaustion approaches

faq: PSU sensor alarm triggered on a server or network device — what actions to take?
likely_causes:
- PSU hardware failure — internal component blown
- Power feed (A/B feed) to PSU disconnected or tripped
- AC inlet voltage out of spec
- PSU overloaded beyond rated wattage
- PSU fan failure causing thermal shutdown
- Firmware bug causing false PSU alarm
observability_signals:
- psu_status == failed
- IPMI/SNMP sensor alarm for PSU
- Syslog: 'PSU failure', 'power supply error'
- Dual-PSU device now on single-feed (redundancy lost)
- Physical amber/red LED on PSU unit
cli_commands:
show environment power (network device)
ipmitool sdr type 'Power Supply'
ipmitool sel list | grep -i psu
show platform hardware chassis
ipmitool power status
rca_steps:
1) Check physical PSU LEDs — amber = fault, green = healthy
2) Verify power feed: is the A/B feed cable connected and PDU breaker not tripped?
3) Run IPMI sensor: 'ipmitool sdr type Power Supply'
4) Check AC inlet voltage within spec (100-240V)
5) Check PSU wattage vs system load — is load within PSU rated capacity?
6) Swap with spare PSU to confirm hardware failure vs feed issue
resolution: Replace failed PSU with RMA unit; restore power feed; trip PDU breaker back; order RMA from vendor.
preventive_action: Monitor PSU status via SNMP; maintain hot-spare PSUs; test power feed failover quarterly; set IPMI PSU alerts.
related_tools: IPMI, SNMP environment MIBs, Syslog, Vendor DCIM
priority: P1 — single point of failure; device at risk of power loss

faq: A critical OS or application service is not running — how to diagnose and restore it?
likely_causes:
- Service crashed due to unhandled exception or OOM kill
- Missing dependency (library, config file, network service)
- Configuration error preventing startup
- Port conflict — another process using required port
- Permission or SELinux/AppArmor denial
- Service not enabled to start after reboot
observability_signals:
- service_running == 0
- Syslog: 'service crashed', 'failed to start'
- systemd journal showing exit code
- Port not in LISTEN state
- Watchdog not detecting and restarting service
cli_commands:
systemctl status <service>
journalctl -u <service> -n 100
ps aux | grep <service>
ss -tlnp | grep <port>
ls -la /var/log/<service>/
strace -p <pid> (if running but misbehaving)
rca_steps:
1) Check service status: 'systemctl status <service>' — exit code and reason
2) Review journal: 'journalctl -u <service>' — last error message
3) Check port conflict: 'ss -tlnp' — is another process on same port?
4) Check dependency: missing config file, DB connection, upstream service?
5) Check SELinux/AppArmor denials: 'ausearch -m AVC' or 'dmesg | grep apparmor'
6) Attempt manual start: '<service> --config /etc/service.conf' to see errors
resolution: Fix configuration; resolve dependency; free port; restart service; re-enable with 'systemctl enable'; fix SELinux context.
preventive_action: Configure systemd restart=always; implement health check with auto-restart; monitor service state via SNMP or Prometheus.
related_tools: systemd, journalctl, SNMP, Prometheus, Nagios
priority: P1 if critical application service; P2 for supporting service

faq: Container is in CrashLoopBackOff — what are the common causes and fixes?
likely_causes:
- Misconfigured environment variable or missing secret
- Upstream dependency (DB, MQ, config service) not ready
- Application code bug causing immediate crash on startup
- Incorrect container entrypoint or command
- Resource limits too restrictive (OOMKilled)
- Image pull failure (ImagePullBackOff)
- Liveness probe misconfigured — killing healthy container
observability_signals:
- container_restarts > 5
- Kubernetes: CrashLoopBackOff or OOMKilled status
- Container exits with non-zero code immediately
- Short container uptime before each restart
- 'Back-off restarting failed container' in events
cli_commands:
kubectl get pods -n <ns>
kubectl describe pod <pod> -n <ns>
kubectl logs <pod> --previous -n <ns>
kubectl get events -n <ns> | grep <pod>
docker inspect <container>
docker logs <container>
rca_steps:
1) Check exit code: 'kubectl describe pod' — OOMKilled=137, crash=1/2/128+n
2) Read previous container logs: 'kubectl logs --previous' — last error before crash
3) Check events: missing secret, image pull failure, probe failure
4) Test dependency reachability from within pod: 'kubectl exec -it <pod> -- curl <dep>'
5) Run image interactively to reproduce: 'docker run -it --entrypoint /bin/sh <image>'
6) Check liveness probe timing — too aggressive for slow-starting apps
resolution: Fix config/secret; add init container for dependency wait; increase resource limits; fix entrypoint; disable liveness probe temporarily to diagnose.
preventive_action: Use readiness probes; implement startup probe for slow apps; set appropriate resource requests/limits; use Helm linting.
related_tools: kubectl, docker, Prometheus, k8s events
priority: P2; P1 if critical production service

faq: Very high PPS traffic rate detected — how to identify and mitigate a DDoS attack?
likely_causes:
- Volumetric UDP/ICMP/SYN flood from spoofed sources
- DNS or NTP amplification using victim's IP as spoofed source
- Botnet-driven HTTP flood
- Exhaustion of connection table or NAT table
- Protocol anomaly attack targeting specific vulnerability
observability_signals:
- pps_rate > 1,000,000
- Interface utilization spiking to near 100%
- CoPP drop counters increasing
- Source IP diversity high (spoofed) or low (botnet with few IPs)
- Protocol mix abnormal (high UDP, ICMP, or SYN)
cli_commands:
show interface counters rate
show ip flow top-talkers
show control-plane statistics
tcpdump -i <int> -c 10000 (capture for analysis)
show access-list <acl> (check ACL hit counters)
show ip bgp dampening
rca_steps:
1) Confirm attack via PPS and protocol analysis — NetFlow top-talkers
2) Identify attack type: volumetric (high bps/pps), protocol (SYN/ICMP), application (HTTP)
3) Identify source: single IP, subnet, or distributed?
4) Apply upstream RTBH if volumetric: 'ip route <victim-prefix> null 0 tag <rtbh-tag>'
5) Rate-limit attack traffic with ACL at edge
6) Contact upstream ISP for scrubbing if throughput exceeds local mitigation capacity
resolution: Apply RTBH for victim prefix; rate-limit at upstream provider; enable scrubbing service; block attack protocol with ACL.
preventive_action: Pre-stage RTBH templates; subscribe to DDoS scrubbing; deploy uRPF (loose mode) on edge; set PPS threshold alerts.
related_tools: NetFlow, SNMP, BGP RTBH, Packet capture, ISP scrubbing
priority: P1 — immediate response required

faq: High rate of connection attempts to many ports from a single source — is this a port scan?
likely_causes:
- External attacker performing reconnaissance before exploitation
- Automated vulnerability scanner (Shodan, Nessus, Nmap)
- Compromised internal host scanning laterally
- Worm propagation attempting to spread
- Misconfigured monitoring tool scanning too aggressively
observability_signals:
- unique_dest_ports > 100 from single source IP in short window
- conn_attempts_per_sec > 200
- High RST/SYN-ACK-RST rate (closed ports responding)
- IDS/IPS alert for reconnaissance pattern
- Firewall deny log showing sequential port progression
cli_commands:
show ip flow top-talkers (src-ip based)
tcpdump -i <int> src <attacker-ip>
show logging | inc 'denied'
netstat -an | grep SYN_RECV
nmap -sV <source-ip> (identify attacker services)
rca_steps:
1) Confirm source IP and port scan pattern via NetFlow/sFlow
2) Check if source is internal (lateral scan) or external
3) If external: apply temporary ACL block for scanning source IP/subnet
4) If internal: isolate host, scan for malware/compromise
5) Determine if legitimate: authorized penetration test? monitoring tool?
6) Review firewall deny logs for what the scanner found open
resolution: Block scanning source at firewall; if internal host compromised: isolate and remediate; coordinate with security team for authorized scans.
preventive_action: Deploy IDS/IPS with port scan signatures; enable firewall connection rate limiting; segment internal networks to limit lateral movement.
related_tools: IDS/IPS, NetFlow, Firewall logs, SIEM
priority: P2 for reconnaissance; P1 if active exploitation detected

faq: High volume of traffic being denied by security policy — could this be a misconfigured rule blocking legitimate flows?
likely_causes:
- ACL or firewall rule added incorrectly blocking legitimate traffic
- Object-group or network object not updated for new IP range
- Rule ordering — deny rule placed above required permit
- Zone-pair missing for new traffic path
- Service object missing port for application update
- Overly broad deny rule during change window
observability_signals:
- policy_denies > 100 per minute
- Specific application or user group reporting connectivity failure
- Firewall deny log showing legitimate 5-tuple being dropped
- Traffic pattern consistent with known application (not attack)
- Issue started after a change window
cli_commands:
show access-list <acl> | inc deny
show conn | inc <src-ip>
packet-tracer input <int> tcp <src> <sport> <dst> <dport> (ASA)
show logging | inc 'deny'
show run object-group | inc <service>
rca_steps:
1) Correlate start of denies with change window — post-change issue?
2) Capture denied 5-tuple from logs: src-ip, dst-ip, src-port, dst-port, protocol
3) Use packet-tracer to simulate the flow through policy
4) Identify which rule is denying: ACL line number, rule name
5) Determine if deny is intentional (security) or accidental (misconfiguration)
6) Roll back specific rule change or insert corrective permit rule above deny
resolution: Insert corrective permit rule above offending deny; update object-group; rollback change; document intended policy.
preventive_action: Use staged policy changes; run packet-tracer before applying rules; maintain change log with rollback plan.
related_tools: Firewall logs, packet-tracer, SIEM, SNMP
priority: P1 if critical application blocked; P2 otherwise

faq: Many failed login attempts from a single source targeting multiple accounts — is this a brute force attack?
likely_causes:
- Automated credential stuffing using leaked password databases
- Targeted brute force against privileged accounts
- Password spray attack (one password tried against many users)
- Compromised internal system performing lateral credential attack
- Misconfigured automation tool retrying with bad credentials
observability_signals:
- auth_failures > 50 in short window
- unique_usernames > 5 targeted from same source IP
- Consistent pattern: same source, sequential account attempts
- High failure rate on specific user names (admin, root, administrator)
- Auth failures from unusual geography or IP range
cli_commands:
show logging | inc 'login failed|auth failure'
tail -f /var/log/auth.log (Linux)
fail2ban-client status (if deployed)
Get-EventLog -LogName Security -EventID 4625 (Windows)
search index=auth sourcetype=linux_secure 'Failed password'
rca_steps:
1) Identify source IP and targeted accounts from auth logs
2) Determine attack type: brute force (one account, many passwords) vs spray (many accounts, one password)
3) Check if source is internal (compromised system) or external
4) Block source IP at firewall immediately
5) Lock accounts with > N failures
6) Check if any account was successfully authenticated after failures — possible breach
resolution: Block source IP; lock targeted accounts; force password reset; deploy/update fail2ban; enable MFA; alert security team.
preventive_action: Deploy MFA; set account lockout after 5 failures; enable geo-blocking; use SIEM for brute force detection rules.
related_tools: SIEM, fail2ban, auth logs, IDS/IPS, AD event logs
priority: P1 if any account successfully compromised; P2 for ongoing attack

faq: Endpoint or network IDS has raised a malware detection alert — what is the response process?
likely_causes:
- Malicious file executed from email attachment or download
- Drive-by web exploit delivering payload
- Lateral movement from already-compromised host
- USB/removable media infection
- Supply chain compromise (malicious software update)
- Living-off-the-land attack using legitimate OS tools
observability_signals:
- malware_alerts > 1 from endpoint AV or IDS/IPS
- Quarantined file detected
- Unusual outbound C2 connections
- Lateral movement indicators (unusual SMB, RDP, WMI activity)
- Registry or scheduled task creation by unknown process
cli_commands:
Get-MpThreatDetection (Windows Defender)
clamscan -r /home --bell (Linux ClamAV)
netstat -anb (check C2 connections)
ps aux | grep -v grep (unusual processes)
autoruns.exe (Windows startup persistence)
wireshark (capture C2 traffic pattern)
rca_steps:
1) Isolate affected host from network immediately — contain blast radius
2) Preserve memory and disk image before remediation
3) Identify malware family from AV signature or hash (VirusTotal)
4) Check for persistence: scheduled tasks, registry run keys, cron
5) Hunt for lateral movement: check logs on nearby hosts
6) Identify initial infection vector (email, download, USB, supply chain)
7) Escalate to incident response team
resolution: Isolate host; collect forensic image; wipe and reimage; restore from clean backup; revoke credentials used by compromised host.
preventive_action: Deploy EDR; enable application whitelisting; block USB; enforce email sandboxing; train users on phishing.
related_tools: EDR, AV, SIEM, VirusTotal, Wireshark, forensic tools
priority: P1 — immediate incident response activation

faq: The RCA engine is returning low-confidence intent matches — how to resolve ambiguous intent mapping?
likely_causes:
- Two or more intents modeled with overlapping signals and keywords
- Insufficient signal diversity to distinguish between similar failure types
- Missing telemetry data leaving key discriminating metrics empty
- Intent trained on insufficient or unbalanced examples
- Signal weights not differentiated enough between competing intents
observability_signals:
- top_intent_score < 0.6
- intent_score_gap < 0.1 between top-2 intents
- Multiple candidate intents in result
- High volume of 'unknown.low_confidence' fallbacks
- Operator overriding engine classification frequently
cli_commands:
Review intent signal definitions in adminData.ts
Check keyword overlap between competing intents
Run test case against classification engine
Review false-positive rate per intent
Analyze operator feedback and manual overrides
rca_steps:
1) Identify which intent pair is conflicting from ambiguity logs
2) Compare signal definitions — do they share the same metrics with similar thresholds?
3) Add discriminating signals unique to each intent
4) Increase weight difference for key discriminating signals
5) Add negative examples to training if ML-based
6) Add intent-specific keywords that don't overlap
resolution: Refine intent signal thresholds; add discriminating metrics; increase weight separation; remove overlapping keywords; retrain or retest.
preventive_action: Run intent collision test suite after each KB update; monitor intent_score_gap distribution; set alert when ambiguity rate exceeds 5%.
related_tools: Intent engine logs, KB admin console, classification test harness
priority: P2 — accuracy issue affecting RCA quality

faq: Many raw alarms are firing but the correlator is not grouping them into situations — why?
likely_causes:
- Topology metadata missing or stale — correlator cannot link events to common parent
- Correlation rules not covering new device types or link types
- Time synchronization issue causing events outside correlation window
- Events from different domains not sharing common entity reference
- Correlator rule thresholds set too conservatively
observability_signals:
- raw_events_clustered < 2 in correlation window
- events_in_window > 20 uncorrelated
- Situation count far below expected during known outage
- Operator creating manual situations frequently
- Topology discovery jobs failing silently
cli_commands:
Check topology discovery job logs
Verify device inventory in CMDB
Review correlation rule definitions
Compare event entity IDs against topology DB
Check NTP sync across event sources
rca_steps:
1) Check topology DB: are all affected devices present with correct relationships?
2) Run topology discovery — is it completing successfully?
3) Check correlation rule coverage: does a rule exist for this event type combination?
4) Verify event timestamps are synchronized (NTP)
5) Check entity ID consistency — do events reference same device ID format?
6) Widen correlation time window as test
resolution: Trigger topology rediscovery; fix CMDB data; create new correlation rule; fix NTP; normalize entity IDs across event sources.
preventive_action: Schedule daily topology discovery; monitor correlation ratio (situations per N events); alert on topology job failures.
related_tools: CMDB, Topology engine, NTP, Event correlation platform
priority: P2 — operator alert fatigue and missed situations

faq: Significant gaps in metric data for a monitored resource — what causes telemetry loss?
likely_causes:
- Metric agent (Prometheus exporter, SNMP agent) stopped on host
- Network path from agent to collector blocked (ACL, firewall)
- Collector capacity exceeded — drops under high ingest load
- Agent configuration changed and no longer scraping target
- Host rebooted without agent auto-restart configured
- SNMP community string changed without updating collector
observability_signals:
- missing_points_percent > 20 over time window
- Scrape failed errors in collector logs
- Exporter process not running on host
- SNMP timeout from collector
- Grafana showing 'No Data' for specific host
cli_commands:
curl http://<host>:9100/metrics (Prometheus node_exporter check)
snmpwalk -v2c -c <community> <host> 1.3.6.1.2.1.1 (SNMP test)
ps aux | grep exporter
systemctl status node_exporter
telnet <host> 9100 (port reachability)
check collector logs: journalctl -u prometheus
rca_steps:
1) Test agent endpoint directly: 'curl http://<host>:<port>/metrics'
2) Check agent process: 'systemctl status <exporter>'
3) Verify network path: telnet to agent port from collector
4) Check collector logs for scrape error messages
5) Validate credentials: SNMP community, API token, certificate
6) Check collector resource utilization — is it overloaded?
resolution: Restart agent; fix firewall ACL; update credentials; scale collector; configure agent to auto-start on boot.
preventive_action: Monitor agent health as a meta-metric; alert on 'exporter down'; configure systemd restart policy for all agents.
related_tools: Prometheus, SNMP, Grafana, systemd, node_exporter
priority: P2 — blind spot in monitoring; may mask outages

faq: Synthetic transaction probes are failing — how to determine if it is a real outage or probe issue?
likely_causes:
- Real application or path degradation detected by probe
- Probe agent itself misconfigured or failing
- Test credentials expired or changed
- Probe timing out due to latency increase (not full failure)
- SSL certificate change breaking probe validation
- Geographic routing change sending probe to degraded POP
observability_signals:
- probe_success_rate < 90% over measurement window
- probe_timeout events in monitoring logs
- Transaction failed with specific HTTP/DNS/TLS error code
- Only specific probe agents failing (agent issue vs real outage)
- Real user traffic showing correlated errors
cli_commands:
curl -o /dev/null -s -w '%{http_code}\n%{time_total}\n' https://<endpoint>
nslookup <endpoint>
openssl s_client -connect <host>:443
traceroute <endpoint>
check probe agent logs
rca_steps:
1) Correlate probe failure with real user impact — is it just the probe or real traffic affected?
2) Run manual curl/ping from probe agent host to target
3) Check if multiple probe agents from different locations are failing — widespread = real outage
4) Check probe configuration: correct URL, port, expected response, timeout value?
5) Validate test credentials still valid
6) Check SSL cert on target — has it changed?
resolution: Fix real outage if confirmed; update probe configuration; renew probe credentials; fix SSL cert; adjust probe timeout.
preventive_action: Deploy probes from multiple geographic locations; correlate synthetic with real-user monitoring; test probe config after any application change.
related_tools: Synthetic monitoring platform, curl, traceroute, APM
priority: P2 (investigate to determine if P1 real outage underneath)

faq: Device chassis temperature is above safe limits — what are the causes and immediate actions?
likely_causes:
- Airflow blocked by cables, blanking panels missing, or rack obstructions
- Fan failure — one or more chassis fans stopped
- CRAC unit not delivering adequate cold air to this rack
- Device placed in wrong airflow orientation (front-to-back vs rear-to-front)
- Ambient room temperature too high
- Chassis air filter clogged
observability_signals:
- temp_c > 70 on chassis sensor
- Fan RPM sensor showing reduced speed or failed fan
- SNMP thermal alarm trap
- Syslog: 'temperature alarm', 'over temperature'
- System throttling CPU to reduce heat output
cli_commands:
show environment temperature (network device)
show environment fans
ipmitool sdr type Temperature
ipmitool sdr type Fan
show platform hardware chassis detail
rca_steps:
1) Check current temperature reading and trend — acute (spike) or chronic (gradual rise)?
2) Inspect all chassis fans: 'show environment fans' or IPMI — any stopped?
3) Check for physical airflow blockage: cables, missing blanking panels, wrong rack orientation
4) Verify CRAC is delivering cold air to this rack zone (inlet temp sensor)
5) Check if neighboring device failure changed airflow patterns
6) Immediate action if > 80°C: consider graceful shutdown before thermal shutdown
resolution: Replace failed fan; clear airflow blockage; install blanking panels; fix CRAC unit; reduce device load to lower heat output.
preventive_action: Monitor all fan sensors; set alert at 65°C; ensure blanking panels in all unused rack slots; clean air filters quarterly.
related_tools: IPMI, SNMP environmental MIBs, DCIM, Syslog
priority: P1 — risk of thermal shutdown and hardware damage

faq: Rack inlet temperature is elevated — how to identify and resolve a hot-spot in the data centre?
likely_causes:
- CRAC unit failure reducing cold air supply to the aisle
- Hot aisle / cold aisle containment breach (blanking panels missing)
- Hotspot in rack due to high-density compute cluster
- Raised floor tiles missing under high-load racks
- HVAC system not keeping up with increased power density
- New high-power equipment added without cooling capacity review
observability_signals:
- inlet_temp_c > 30 at rack temperature sensor
- Nearby device chassis temperatures also rising
- CRAC discharge temperature higher than normal
- Airflow pattern showing recirculation (hot exhaust mixing with cold inlet)
- Power draw increase in this rack
cli_commands:
ipmitool sdr type Temperature (multiple devices in rack)
check DCIM rack power/temperature dashboard
check CRAC unit telemetry
measure inlet temp physically with calibrated probe
rca_steps:
1) Identify which rack(s) are affected using per-rack inlet sensors
2) Check CRAC unit status serving the affected zone
3) Physical inspection: missing blanking panels? cables blocking airflow?
4) Check containment integrity: hot aisle containment doors sealed?
5) Review recent power additions to the rack — new high-power equipment?
6) Measure CRAC discharge temperature — is it delivering cold air?
resolution: Restore CRAC operation; install missing blanking panels; seal containment; redistribute high-density equipment; add supplemental cooling.
preventive_action: Regular airflow audits; capacity review before adding high-power equipment; deploy per-rack temperature monitoring.
related_tools: DCIM, CRAC telemetry, rack temperature sensors, IPMI
priority: P1 if multiple devices at risk; P2 for early warning

faq: CRAC/CRAH unit is reporting a fault — what is the response and remediation process?
likely_causes:
- Compressor failure in CRAC unit
- Refrigerant leak reducing cooling capacity
- CRAC fan motor failure
- Water leak or condensate drain blockage
- Loss of power to CRAC unit
- Control board fault or firmware issue
- Filter clogged reducing airflow through CRAC
observability_signals:
- crac_status == fault
- CRAC alarm on BMS (Building Management System)
- Rising inlet temperatures in affected zone
- CRAC discharge air temperature increasing
- Audible alarm from CRAC unit
cli_commands:
Check BMS/DCIM for CRAC alarm detail
Inspect CRAC local panel for error code
Check facility power feed to CRAC
Review CRAC vendor management interface
Check temperature trend since CRAC fault time
rca_steps:
1) Identify fault type from CRAC local panel or BMS error code
2) Check power feed to CRAC — is it receiving power?
3) Inspect physical unit: water leak? ice buildup? fan running?
4) Assess impact: how many racks served by this CRAC? what is current inlet temp trend?
5) Activate emergency cooling (portable units, open containment) to buy time
6) Engage CRAC vendor for emergency field service
resolution: Emergency portable cooling; engage vendor for CRAC repair; evacuate high-density equipment if temperature continues rising; activate backup CRAC if available.
preventive_action: N+1 CRAC redundancy; quarterly CRAC maintenance; deploy per-unit monitoring; test backup CRAC activation annually.
related_tools: BMS, DCIM, CRAC vendor portal, SNMP environmental MIBs
priority: P1 — data centre thermal risk

faq: Device is running on a single power feed after losing PSU redundancy — what are the risks and actions?
likely_causes:
- One PSU in dual-PSU device has failed
- A-feed or B-feed power strip (PDU) tripped or failed
- Facility breaker tripped on one power feed
- Power cable disconnected from PSU accidentally
- UPS failure on one power feed path
observability_signals:
- psu_redundant == 0 (redundancy lost)
- IPMI sensor showing one PSU failed
- SNMP PSU redundancy alarm
- Physical amber LED on failed PSU
- BMS showing PDU circuit fault
cli_commands:
show environment power (Cisco)
ipmitool sdr type 'Power Supply'
ipmitool sel list | grep -i 'power\|psu'
check PDU outlet status (via PDU SNMP/REST)
show platform power chassis (vendor-specific)
rca_steps:
1) Identify which PSU has failed from IPMI or chassis LEDs
2) Trace power path: PSU → power cable → PDU outlet → facility breaker → UPS
3) Check PDU outlet: is it providing power? (PDU SNMP/REST API)
4) Check facility breaker panel for tripped breaker on that feed
5) If PSU hardware failed: plan emergency replacement (hot-swap if supported)
6) Assess risk window: how long can device run single-feed before next action?
resolution: Replace failed PSU (hot-swap); restore tripped PDU/breaker; reconnect power cable; order RMA if PSU hardware fault.
preventive_action: Monitor PSU redundancy via SNMP; test A/B feed failover quarterly; ensure A and B feeds from independent PDUs and UPS paths.
related_tools: IPMI, PDU SNMP, DCIM, BMS, SNMP environmental MIBs
priority: P1 — single point of failure for device power

faq: Disk IOPS are far below expected capacity and queue depth is high — what is causing storage performance degradation?
likely_causes:
- Failing disk causing retries and slow I/O
- RAID rebuild consuming I/O bandwidth (degraded array)
- Backend SAN/NAS array under heavy contention from other hosts
- Storage network (FC/iSCSI) congestion
- LUN not on SSD tier — migrated to slower tier
- Snapshot overhead consuming IOPS
- Host-side I/O scheduler or driver misconfiguration
observability_signals:
- disk_iops < 500 when expected > 5000
- disk_queue_depth > 20 (I/O requests queuing)
- High await time in iostat
- Storage controller showing busy/overloaded
cli_commands:
iostat -xz 1 10 (Linux)
iostat -d -x <device> (per-disk)
sar -d 1 30
fio --filename=<dev> --rw=randread --bs=4k --runtime=10 (synthetic test)
vmstat 1 10 (wa column)
check array management console for disk/RAID status
rca_steps:
1) Run iostat: identify device with high await and low IOPS
2) Check if RAID is degraded: array console or 'cat /proc/mdstat'
3) Check disk health: 'smartctl -a /dev/sdX' for reallocated sectors
4) Test isolated disk IOPS with fio to confirm degradation
5) Check array management for other hosts consuming same storage pool
6) Review snapshot schedule — is a snapshot running during peak?
resolution: Replace failing disk; complete RAID rebuild on new disk; balance storage load across pools; move LUN to SSD tier; defer snapshots to off-peak.
preventive_action: Monitor SMART attributes daily; set IOPS and latency alert thresholds; stagger RAID rebuilds; review snapshot schedules.
related_tools: iostat, smartctl, fio, Array management console, SNMP
priority: P1 if database or critical application I/O affected

faq: A storage volume is above 90% utilization — what is consuming space and how to recover?
likely_causes:
- Log files growing without rotation or retention policy
- Database data or temp files growing unexpectedly
- Backup files accumulating past retention period
- Core dumps filling /var/crash or similar
- Container or VM disk image growth
- Snapshot reserve space consumed
observability_signals:
- volume_used_percent > 90
- Filesystem at 100% causing write failures
- Application errors: 'no space left on device'
- Log rotation failing (cannot write new log)
- Database unable to grow data file
cli_commands:
df -h (filesystem usage)
du -sh /* | sort -rh | head -20 (top consumers)
find / -name 'core' -size +100M (core dumps)
find /var/log -name '*.log' -size +1G
du -sh /var/log/* | sort -rh
crontab -l | grep logrotate
rca_steps:
1) Identify top space consumer: 'du -sh /* | sort -rh'
2) Check /var/log for uncleaned logs
3) Check backup directory for files past retention period
4) Look for core dumps: 'find / -name core'
5) Check database temp/data file growth
6) Immediate emergency space: delete old rotated logs, expired backups, core dumps
resolution: Free space immediately (delete logs/cores/expired backups); fix log rotation; expand volume; add storage; enforce retention policies.
preventive_action: Set alert at 75%/85%; enforce log rotation with logrotate; set backup retention limits; capacity trend reporting.
related_tools: df, du, logrotate, SNMP, DCIM capacity management
priority: P1 if volume full causing write failures; P2 at 90%

faq: Storage snapshot jobs are failing repeatedly — what are the common causes and fixes?
likely_causes:
- Insufficient snapshot reserve space on volume
- Snapshot schedule overlapping — previous snapshot not yet complete
- Volume locked by application during snapshot quiesce
- Snapshot target storage exhausted
- Permissions issue — snapshot agent lacks necessary privilege
- Array firmware bug in snapshot subsystem
observability_signals:
- snapshot_failures > 1 in monitoring window
- Snapshot job error in storage management console
- Backup SLA missed due to snapshot failure
- Volume snapshot reserve percentage high
- 'Insufficient space' or 'lock timeout' in snapshot logs
cli_commands:
Check array snapshot management console
df -h (check snapshot reserve on volume)
vgdisplay / lvdisplay (LVM snapshot space)
check backup application logs
check snapshot schedule for overlaps
array CLI: snap list / snap status (vendor-specific)
rca_steps:
1) Check snapshot error message for specific failure type (space, lock, timeout)
2) Check snapshot reserve: is it fully consumed by existing snapshots?
3) Delete expired snapshots to free reserve space
4) Check if application quiesce script is timing out — app too slow to quiesce?
5) Verify snapshot schedule does not overlap (previous not finished when next starts)
6) Check snapshot agent credentials and privileges
resolution: Expand snapshot reserve; delete expired snapshots; stagger schedule; fix quiesce script; grant correct permissions; vendor firmware patch.
preventive_action: Alert when snapshot reserve > 70%; test snapshot schedule in maintenance window; monitor quiesce success rate.
related_tools: Storage array management console, backup agent logs, SNMP
priority: P2; P1 if backup SLA and RPO at risk

faq: Storage I/O latency has spiked — how to identify the source of the delay?
likely_causes:
- Backend storage array under heavy concurrent load from multiple hosts
- SAN fabric congestion causing frame drops
- Storage controller cache flushing (write-back cache full)
- Failing disk causing retries adding latency
- Snapshot or replication activity competing for array resources
- QoS policy on array throttling specific LUN
observability_signals:
- io_latency_ms > 20 for database or critical application LUN
- High await in iostat on host
- Array controller showing queue depth high
- SAN fabric showing congestion or credit starvation
- Other hosts on same array also showing latency
cli_commands:
iostat -x 1 10 (host-side latency: await column)
sar -d 1 60
fio --rw=randread --bs=4k --runtime=30 (test latency directly)
check SAN switch: show fcns database, show interface counters
check array queue depth and latency from array console
netstat -s | grep retransmit (if iSCSI)
rca_steps:
1) Measure host-side latency: iostat await on the LUN device
2) Compare latency on array: is it array-side or host/HBA side?
3) Check SAN fabric: FC credit starvation? congestion? (show interface counters on FC switch)
4) Identify other hosts using same storage pool — is it shared contention?
5) Check for snapshot or replication running during latency spike
6) Review array QoS — is LUN throttled?
resolution: Balance workloads across storage pools; defer snapshots; expand SAN bandwidth; upgrade array cache; disable array QoS throttle.
preventive_action: Set latency alert at 10ms; monitor array queue depth; isolate OLTP LUNs from batch workloads; size SAN fabric for peak concurrent I/O.
related_tools: iostat, sar, fio, SAN management console, array management
priority: P1 for database/application-impacting latency

faq: Storage replication is lagging — what is causing the replication delay and how to catch up?
likely_causes:
- Insufficient bandwidth on replication link for write rate
- Target array too slow to ingest replicated I/O
- Replication link experiencing high latency or packet loss
- High write rate on source exceeding replication throughput
- Replication paused and resuming with large backlog
- QoS throttle on replication traffic
observability_signals:
- replication_lag_sec > 300 (5 minutes behind)
- Replication link utilization near 100%
- Target array write latency high
- Syslog: 'replication behind', 'mirror out of sync'
- RPO SLA breach alert
cli_commands:
Check array replication status: show replication (vendor-specific)
iperf3 -c <replication-target-ip> (test replication link bandwidth)
ping <replication-target> (check latency)
Check replication link interface utilization
Check target array write latency
Check replication QoS policy on array
rca_steps:
1) Determine current lag size and trend — is it growing or stable?
2) Check replication link utilization — is it saturated?
3) Test raw link bandwidth with iperf3 vs expected write rate
4) Check target array ingest rate — is target array the bottleneck?
5) Review source write rate — has a bulk load or backup increased write rate?
6) Check if replication was paused and is recovering backlog
resolution: Increase replication link bandwidth; throttle source workload temporarily; scale target array; increase replication thread count; upgrade replication link.
preventive_action: Size replication link to 120% of peak write rate; set RPO alert before breach; test replication recovery time quarterly.
related_tools: Array management console, iperf3, SNMP, replication monitoring
priority: P1 if RPO SLA being breached; P2 for early warning

faq: Database is not reachable — how to determine if it is the process, host, or network?
likely_causes:
- Database process crashed or OOM killed
- Host running the DB is down or unreachable
- Database listener not started after maintenance
- Database port blocked by firewall or ACL change
- Disk full preventing DB from writing (crash on write failure)
- Corrupt control file or redo log causing startup failure
observability_signals:
- db_up == 0
- Connection refused or timeout to DB port
- Host ICMP unreachable
- DB process not in process list
- OOM kill event in kernel log coinciding with DB crash
cli_commands:
ping <db_host>
telnet <db_host> <db_port> (e.g. 5432, 3306, 1521)
ps aux | grep postgres|mysql|oracle
systemctl status postgresql|mysql
tail -100 /var/log/postgresql/postgresql*.log
df -h (check disk space)
journalctl -u postgresql -n 200
rca_steps:
1) Is host reachable? Ping — if no: escalate to infrastructure/host team
2) Is DB port listening? Telnet to port — if no: process down
3) Check DB process: 'systemctl status' and 'ps aux | grep db'
4) Check DB error log for crash reason: OOM? disk full? corrupt files?
5) Check disk space: 'df -h' — full disk causes DB write failure and crash
6) Attempt DB startup and capture error output
resolution: Restart DB process; free disk space; repair corrupt files; restore from backup if corruption is severe; fix network/firewall if connectivity issue.
preventive_action: Set DB availability probe; alert on disk > 85%; enable DB crash dump analysis; configure DB auto-restart via systemd.
related_tools: Prometheus DB exporter, pg_stat_activity, MySQL status, SNMP, journalctl
priority: P1 — critical service unavailable

faq: High rate of slow queries detected — what are the common causes and tuning approach?
likely_causes:
- Missing index on frequently queried column (full table scan)
- Stale statistics causing optimizer to choose wrong plan
- N+1 query pattern in application (ORM issue)
- Lock contention causing queries to wait
- Insufficient DB server memory causing cache eviction
- Long-running transactions blocking newer queries
observability_signals:
- slow_queries_per_min > 20
- Query execution time > threshold (e.g., > 1s)
- Full table scan indicator in query plan
- Lock wait time increasing
- DB buffer cache hit ratio decreasing
cli_commands:
SHOW FULL PROCESSLIST; (MySQL)
SELECT query, total_time FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY total_time DESC LIMIT 10; (PostgreSQL)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE <slow_query>;
SELECT * FROM sys.dm_exec_query_stats (SQL Server)
SHOW STATUS LIKE 'Slow_queries'; (MySQL)
SHOW ENGINE INNODB STATUS; (MySQL locks)
rca_steps:
1) Identify slowest queries from slow query log or pg_stat_statements
2) Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on top slow query — look for seq scan, hash join on large table
3) Check for missing index: pg_stat_user_tables seq_scan high
4) Look for lock contention: long-running transactions blocking others
5) Check DB memory: buffer cache hit ratio < 95% indicates insufficient RAM
6) Check if statistics are stale: ANALYZE/UPDATE STATISTICS
resolution: Add missing index; update statistics; kill long-running locks; add DB server RAM; fix N+1 in application; optimize query.
preventive_action: Enable slow query log; monitor pg_stat_statements weekly; index coverage review after schema changes; load test before deployment.
related_tools: pg_stat_statements, MySQL slow query log, EXPLAIN, APM, DB monitoring
priority: P2; P1 if causing application timeout or outage

faq: Database replica is lagging behind primary — what causes replica lag and how to resolve?
likely_causes:
- Replica hardware weaker than primary — cannot keep up with write rate
- Long-running read queries on replica blocking replication thread
- Network latency between primary and replica
- Parallel replication not enabled — single-threaded apply
- Large transactions on primary creating huge binlog events
- Replica I/O thread paused or errored
observability_signals:
- db_replication_delay_sec > 120
- Replica 'Seconds_Behind_Master' > 120 (MySQL)
- pg_stat_replication replay_lag > 120s (PostgreSQL)
- Replica I/O thread: Waiting for master to send event
- Replica SQL thread: applying events slowly
cli_commands:
SHOW SLAVE STATUS\G; (MySQL)
SELECT * FROM pg_stat_replication; (PostgreSQL)
SHOW REPLICA STATUS\G; (MySQL 8+)
Check replica server iostat and CPU
SELECT pid, query, state FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state != 'idle'; (blocking queries on replica)
rca_steps:
1) Check replica lag: 'SHOW SLAVE STATUS' — Seconds_Behind_Master value
2) Check if I/O or SQL thread is the bottleneck
3) Is replica I/O bound? Check iostat on replica during replication
4) Enable parallel replication (MySQL: slave_parallel_workers=4)
5) Check for long-running queries on replica blocking SQL thread
6) Verify network bandwidth between primary and replica
resolution: Enable parallel replication; upgrade replica hardware; kill blocking queries on replica; compress replication stream; dedicate replica CPU to replication.
preventive_action: Monitor replica lag continuously; set alert at 60s lag; load test replica before promoting; use async replication for analytics replicas.
related_tools: MySQL SHOW SLAVE STATUS, pg_stat_replication, Prometheus DB exporter
priority: P2; P1 if replica is production read target or near failover threshold

faq: Database tablespace is near full — how to identify what is growing and how to add space?
likely_causes:
- Table data growing faster than expected (business growth or data ingestion rate)
- Index fragmentation consuming extra space
- Undo/rollback segment growing due to long-running transactions
- Temporary tablespace not reclaiming space after large operations
- Audit or log table not purged
- Bloat from frequent updates/deletes without VACUUM (PostgreSQL)
observability_signals:
- tablespace_used_percent > 90
- DB error: 'unable to extend segment'
- No space left in tablespace
- DBA alert for tablespace threshold breach
cli_commands:
SELECT tablespace_name, used_space, tablespace_size FROM dba_tablespace_usage_metrics; (Oracle)
SELECT pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size('<db>')); (PostgreSQL)
SELECT table_name, data_length+index_length FROM information_schema.tables ORDER BY 3 DESC; (MySQL)
SELECT object_name, bytes FROM dba_segments ORDER BY bytes DESC FETCH FIRST 20 ROWS ONLY; (Oracle)
ANALYZE table_name; VACUUM ANALYZE table_name; (PostgreSQL)
rca_steps:
1) Identify largest segments/tables consuming tablespace
2) Check undo/temp tablespace — are long-running transactions holding undo?
3) Kill long-running transactions consuming undo space
4) Run PostgreSQL VACUUM ANALYZE to reclaim bloat
5) Purge old audit/log tables
6) Add a new datafile to tablespace as emergency measure
resolution: Add datafile; purge old data; run VACUUM; kill long transactions; archive and delete old tables; expand tablespace storage.
preventive_action: Set tablespace alert at 75%/85%; schedule weekly VACUUM/purge; monitor top-growth tables; capacity plan for projected data growth.
related_tools: Oracle DBA views, pg_stat_user_tables, MySQL information_schema, DCIM storage capacity
priority: P1 if writes failing; P2 at 90% approaching full

faq: Database backup job has failed — what are the causes and how to ensure RPO compliance?
likely_causes:
- Backup target storage full or unreachable
- Backup agent credentials expired or wrong
- DB user for backup lacking necessary privileges
- Backup software conflict with DB version
- Backup window timeout — DB backup too large for window
- Network failure during backup transfer to remote target
- DB in inconsistent state preventing consistent backup
observability_signals:
- db_backup_success == 0
- Backup job error in scheduler (cron, backup software)
- Backup storage volume near full
- Network error in backup transfer logs
- Backup verification failure (corrupted backup)
cli_commands:
Check backup scheduler logs (cron, Veeam, Commvault)
pg_dumpall -v > /dev/null (PostgreSQL test)
mysqldump --single-transaction -u <user> -p <db> > /dev/null (MySQL test)
df -h <backup-target> (backup storage space)
ping <backup-target-host> (network reachability)
check backup agent logs in /var/log/backup_agent.log
rca_steps:
1) Check backup job error message — specific failure reason
2) Test backup manually to reproduce error
3) Check backup target storage: 'df -h <target>'
4) Test network reachability to backup target
5) Verify DB credentials for backup have required permissions
6) Check if DB is in a state allowing consistent backup (not mid-transaction)
resolution: Free backup storage; fix credentials; grant backup permissions; extend backup window; fix network path; trigger manual backup after fix.
preventive_action: Monitor backup success daily; alert on first failure immediately; test restore quarterly; set backup storage alert at 75%.
related_tools: Backup scheduler logs, backup software console, SNMP, storage monitoring
priority: P1 — RPO SLA risk

faq: Message queue depth is above threshold — is the consumer too slow or is there a producer surge?
likely_causes:
- Consumer application crashed or stopped
- Consumer processing too slow for message rate (under-scaled)
- Poison message causing consumer to fail and requeue repeatedly
- Consumer backpressure due to downstream dependency (DB, API) slow
- Producer surge — sudden spike in message production rate
- Queue depth limit reached — oldest messages being dropped
observability_signals:
- queue_depth > 1000 and growing
- Consumer throughput (messages/sec) lower than producer rate
- Consumer lag increasing in Kafka monitoring
- Dead-letter queue (DLQ) accumulating poison messages
- Consumer CPU/memory normal (consumer not processing, not crashed)
cli_commands:
rabbitmqctl list_queues name messages consumers (RabbitMQ)
kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe --group <group> (Kafka)
aws sqs get-queue-attributes --attribute-names All (SQS)
check consumer application logs
kafka-topics.sh --describe --topic <topic> (partition replication)
rca_steps:
1) Check consumer count: is it running? 'rabbitmqctl list_queues' consumers column = 0?
2) Check consumer throughput vs producer rate — is consumer keeping up?
3) Inspect consumer logs for errors — is it crashing on a specific message?
4) Check DLQ for poison messages — requeue count > N indicates poison message
5) Check downstream dependency (DB, API) — consumer blocked waiting for it?
6) For Kafka: check partition leadership and rebalancing events
resolution: Scale consumer instances; fix poison message (DLQ analysis); fix downstream dependency; increase consumer thread count; trigger rebalance.
preventive_action: Monitor consumer lag continuously; set queue depth alert; implement DLQ with max-retry; autoscale consumers on lag threshold.
related_tools: RabbitMQ management, Kafka consumer group monitor, AWS SQS, Prometheus JMX exporter
priority: P2; P1 if queue full and messages being dropped

faq: Message broker is unreachable from clients — how to identify whether it is a process or network failure?
likely_causes:
- Broker process crashed (OOM, uncaught exception)
- Disk full preventing broker from writing journal/data files
- Network connectivity between clients and broker lost
- Broker port blocked by firewall change
- Cluster split-brain — broker ejected from cluster
- TLS certificate expired blocking TLS connections
observability_signals:
- broker_up == 0
- Connection refused or timeout on broker port
- Client libraries showing connection error
- Broker process not running on host
- Broker disk full (check data directory)
cli_commands:
telnet <broker-host> <port> (e.g. 5672 AMQP, 9092 Kafka, 1883 MQTT)
ps aux | grep rabbitmq|kafka|mosquitto
systemctl status rabbitmq-server|kafka
df -h /var/lib/rabbitmq or /kafka-data
rabbitmqctl status
kafka-broker-api-versions.sh --bootstrap-server <host>:9092
rca_steps:
1) Is host reachable: ping broker host
2) Is broker port open: telnet to broker port
3) Check broker process: systemctl status and ps aux
4) If process down: check broker log for crash reason and disk space
5) If cluster: check cluster membership — is broker partitioned from cluster?
6) Check TLS: openssl s_client for TLS brokers (MQTT TLS, RabbitMQ with TLS)
resolution: Restart broker process; free disk space; fix firewall; restore cluster membership; renew TLS certificate.
preventive_action: Broker availability probe (synthetic); alert on disk > 80% for broker data directory; cluster health monitoring; certificate expiry monitoring.
related_tools: RabbitMQ management API, Kafka admin CLI, SNMP, Prometheus broker exporter
priority: P1 — all messaging halted for affected topics/queues

faq: Application transaction error rate is elevated — how to isolate whether it is the app, DB, or downstream service?
likely_causes:
- Downstream microservice returning errors (5xx)
- Database connection pool exhausted — new transactions cannot get a connection
- Timeout on downstream API call — circuit breaker open
- Application bug introduced in recent deployment
- Infrastructure overload (CPU, memory) slowing processing
- External third-party API degradation
observability_signals:
- txn_error_rate_percent > 5
- Specific error codes: HTTP 500, 503, timeout
- Downstream dependency latency increased
- DB connection pool near maximum
- Circuit breaker in open state in APM
cli_commands:
curl -o /dev/null -s -w '%{http_code}\n' https://<service>/health
check APM traces for error-contributing service
check DB connection pool metrics
check downstream service health endpoints
review application logs: grep 'ERROR\|Exception' app.log
check recent deployment history
rca_steps:
1) Identify which error code is failing: 500 (app), 503 (capacity), timeout (latency)
2) Check APM distributed trace to identify which service/call is failing
3) Is failure correlated with a recent deployment? Rollback if so
4) Check downstream service health: API health endpoints, their error rate
5) Check DB connection pool: is it exhausted?
6) Check infrastructure resources: CPU, memory on affected service
resolution: Rollback bad deployment; fix downstream dependency; increase DB connection pool; add service capacity; fix circuit breaker configuration.
preventive_action: Implement circuit breakers and bulkheads; canary deploy; distributed tracing; set error rate alert at 1% for early warning.
related_tools: APM, distributed tracing, Prometheus, Grafana, health check endpoints
priority: P1 if customer-facing; P2 for internal services

faq: Load balancer VIP is down — is it the VIP configuration, the pool members, or the LB itself?
likely_causes:
- All pool members failing health checks
- VIP administratively disabled
- Load balancer itself unreachable (hardware or network failure)
- Virtual server configuration deleted or corrupted
- License limit reached preventing VIP from becoming active
- Routing issue — VIP IP not reachable from clients
observability_signals:
- vip_up == 0
- All pool members in DOWN state
- Health check failure logs for all members
- VIP traffic counters at zero
- Client receiving connection refused or timeout on VIP IP
cli_commands:
show ltm virtual <vip> (F5)
show run | sec virtual-server (F5 / Citrix)
ping <vip-ip> from client subnet
curl -o /dev/null -s -w '%{http_code}\n' http://<vip-ip>/health
show node (F5) — check all pool member states
check health monitor logs
rca_steps:
1) Test VIP connectivity from client: ping and curl
2) Check LB management console: VIP status and pool member states
3) Are all pool members down? Check health check failure reason (TCP failure, HTTP non-2xx, timeout)
4) Test pool member directly (bypassing LB): curl to member IP
5) If members are healthy directly: health check misconfiguration on LB
6) Is VIP administratively disabled? Check audit log for disable action
resolution: Fix pool member application; correct health check config; re-enable VIP; fix routing to VIP; restore LB hardware/config.
preventive_action: Monitor VIP availability via synthetic probe; alert on pool member count < minimum; test health checks match application reality.
related_tools: LB management console, curl, ping, synthetic monitoring, SNMP
priority: P1 — complete service outage

faq: More than 50% of LB pool members are down — how to diagnose and restore pool capacity?
likely_causes:
- Backend application crash on multiple hosts (code bug, OOM)
- Deployment failed — new version broken on most hosts
- Health check too strict — passing threshold lowered
- Network issue between LB and pool member subnet
- DB or upstream dependency down — app failing health check
- OS-level service crash across fleet (e.g., systemd service down)
observability_signals:
- pool_down_members_percent > 50
- Health check failure rate spiked across all members simultaneously
- Application error logs on member hosts
- Event correlation: deployment or config change before member failures
- DB or external dependency failure coinciding with pool member drops
cli_commands:
show pool <pool> members (F5 / Citrix)
curl http://<member-ip>:<port>/healthz (test each member)
check application logs on member hosts
check recent deployment logs (CI/CD)
check DB connectivity from member host
rca_steps:
1) Identify if failure is simultaneous or rolling — simultaneous suggests deployment or dependency
2) Test individual member health check endpoint directly from LB
3) SSH to a failed member — is the app running? Any error logs?
4) Correlate with recent deployment: was a new version pushed?
5) Check upstream dependency: DB, config service, auth service
6) If deployment: rollback; if dependency: fix dependency first
resolution: Rollback deployment; fix dependency; restart crashed service; scale up healthy members; fix health check if false positive.
preventive_action: Blue/green or canary deployments; minimum healthy member threshold alert; synthetic probes per member; pre-deployment health baseline.
related_tools: LB management console, CI/CD logs, APM, application logs, Prometheus
priority: P1 if service degraded; P1+ if VIP also down

faq: Clients experiencing connection resets from the load balancer — what are the causes?
likely_causes:
- LB idle timeout shorter than application session duration
- Pool member sending TCP RST after LB keeps connection alive
- Asymmetric session handling — LB and member session tables out of sync
- Connection table on LB exhausted (max connections reached)
- LB sending RST for half-open connections
- SSL session resumption disabled causing unnecessary renegotiation
observability_signals:
- session_resets_per_min > 50
- Client-visible connection reset errors
- LB connection table utilization high
- TCP RST packets visible in capture on LB egress
- Session duration shorter than application expected
cli_commands:
show ltm virtual <vip> stats (connection table: F5)
tcpdump -i <int> 'tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-rst != 0' (RST capture)
show conn table (ASA/FWSM)
check LB idle timeout setting
check member-side TCP keepalive setting
netstat -an | grep ESTABLISHED | wc -l (connection count)
rca_steps:
1) Capture RST packets to confirm LB is source
2) Check LB idle timeout vs application session keep-alive period
3) Check LB connection table utilization — near max?
4) Test with increased idle timeout and observe session drop rate
5) Verify member-side TCP keepalive is enabled and interval < LB timeout
6) Check for asymmetric routing causing state table miss
resolution: Increase LB idle timeout; enable TCP keepalive on members; scale LB connection table; fix asymmetric routing; tune connection persistence.
preventive_action: Document idle timeout requirements per application; set connection table utilization alert; test session persistence during LB maintenance.
related_tools: LB management console, Wireshark/tcpdump, SNMP, connection metrics
priority: P2; P1 if impacting long-running sessions (streaming, file transfers)

faq: High rate of SSL/TLS handshake errors on the load balancer — what are the causes?
likely_causes:
- SSL certificate expired or approaching expiry
- Certificate CN/SAN mismatch with client-requested hostname
- Cipher suite mismatch between client and LB
- TLS version mismatch (client using TLS 1.0 but LB requiring 1.2+)
- Intermediate CA chain not served by LB
- Client certificate required but not presented (mTLS misconfiguration)
- HSM or SSL offload card fault
observability_signals:
- ssl_handshake_errors_per_min > 20
- Client errors: SSL_ERROR_HANDSHAKE_FAILURE
- LB SSL error logs: handshake failure, certificate expired
- Certificate expiry date past or imminent
- Specific client versions or browsers failing (cipher subset issue)
cli_commands:
