openssl s_client -connect <vip>:443 -servername <hostname>
openssl x509 -noout -dates -in <cert>
curl -v https://<vip>/ (full TLS handshake debug)
openssl ciphers -v 'HIGH:!aNULL' (LB cipher list)
check LB SSL profile: 'show ltm profile client-ssl <profile>' (F5)
openssl verify -CAfile <chain.pem> <cert.pem>
rca_steps:
1) Test TLS handshake: 'openssl s_client -connect <vip>:443' — observe error
2) Check certificate expiry: 'openssl x509 -noout -dates'
3) Verify CN/SAN matches requested hostname
4) Check chain: intermediate CA included in LB SSL profile?
5) Test with different TLS versions: 'openssl s_client -tls1_2'
6) Compare client cipher list with LB cipher list — any intersection?
resolution: Renew/replace certificate; update CN/SAN; add intermediate CA to chain; update cipher list; set minimum TLS version; fix mTLS config.
preventive_action: Automate certificate renewal (ACME); set 30/60 day expiry alerts; include cipher compatibility in client acceptance testing.
related_tools: openssl, curl, LB management console, certificate management platform
priority: P1 if HTTPS service broken for all clients

faq: Requests are being routed to the wrong backend pool — how to diagnose L7 routing rule issues?
likely_causes:
- L7 routing rule condition incorrect (wrong host header, path, method)
- Rule order incorrect — default catch-all matching before specific rule
- New routing rule added without testing conflicting with existing rule
- Policy applied to wrong virtual server
- URL rewrite rule modifying path in unintended way
- Application returning wrong response causing health check to send to wrong pool
observability_signals:
- policy_mismatch_hits > 10 — default pool being used unexpectedly
- Application receiving requests not intended for it
- Specific URL paths returning wrong application responses
- Requests for /api/* going to frontend pool instead of API pool
cli_commands:
show ltm policy <policy> (F5)
curl -H 'Host: <hostname>' http://<vip>/<path> -v (test routing)
check iRules or routing rules in LB console
show run ltm rule (F5 iRule)
check access logs on unintended backend (requests arriving there)
rca_steps:
1) Test routing manually with curl: does specific path/host go to correct pool?
2) Review L7 rules in order — identify which rule is matching
3) Check rule condition: is regex/wildcard too broad?
4) Check rule ordering — more specific rules must precede catch-all
5) Use LB policy trace/debug mode to see which rule matches
6) Compare current rules with last known-good configuration
resolution: Fix rule condition; reorder rules (specific before generic); remove conflicting rule; rollback to previous policy version.
preventive_action: Test routing rules with comprehensive URL matrix before deploying; use LB policy version control; add rule change to change management.
related_tools: LB management console, curl, access logs, policy diff tool
priority: P2; P1 if security-sensitive routing is wrong

faq: SD-WAN underlay transport is showing high latency and loss — how to confirm ISP degradation and trigger path switching?
likely_causes:
- ISP network congestion or peering issue on the underlay path
- Last-mile access circuit degraded (DSL sync issue, fiber fault)
- ISP routing change increasing path latency
- QoS on underlay not preserving DSCP markings of SD-WAN probes
- WAN interface physical errors increasing latency
- ISP BGP route instability causing longer paths
observability_signals:
- transport_latency_ms > 100 on affected path
- transport_loss_percent > 2 on affected path
- SD-WAN controller showing SLA violation on path
- BFD probes increasing RTT on underlay
- Other paths to same site showing normal latency
cli_commands:
show app-route stats (Cisco SD-WAN)
show sdwan bfd sessions (latency/loss per tunnel)
ping <underlay-peer> source <local-transport>
traceroute <underlay-peer> (identify where latency added)
show interface <wan-int> counters (physical errors?)
check ISP circuit status portal
rca_steps:
1) Compare all transport paths — is degradation on one or all paths to site?
2) Run traceroute to identify which hop is adding latency
3) Check physical WAN interface: errors, optical levels (fiber)
4) Confirm SD-WAN SLA class is detecting the violation
5) Verify path switching is occurring — app traffic should move to healthy path
6) Open ISP ticket with latency/loss evidence (timestamps, traceroute)
resolution: SD-WAN should auto-switch to healthy path if SLA class configured; engage ISP; if no healthy path: escalate circuit fault.
preventive_action: Configure SLA classes with appropriate loss/latency thresholds; test path failover quarterly; monitor per-path KPIs continuously.
related_tools: SD-WAN controller, IPSLA, traceroute, ISP portal, SNMP
priority: P2 if path switching is working; P1 if all paths degraded

faq: SD-WAN overlay tunnel is repeatedly flapping — what are the causes and how to stabilize?
likely_causes:
- Underlay transport instability causing keepalive packet loss
- BFD timers too aggressive for high-latency underlay
- NAT mapping changing on underlay (dynamic CGNAT)
- Tunnel keepalive timeout too short for underlay jitter
- DTLS/IPsec re-key issues
- Control connection to vSmart/controller unstable
observability_signals:
- tunnel_state_changes > 3 in monitoring window
- BFD session flapping on tunnel
- Route withdrawals in SD-WAN routing table during flap
- Application traffic impacted with each flap (brief outage)
- Underlay loss/latency elevated before tunnel flap
cli_commands:
show sdwan tunnel statistics (Cisco vEdge)
show sdwan bfd sessions detail
show sdwan omp peers
show sdwan control connections
check underlay ping loss between edge devices
show crypto ipsec sa (if IPsec-based)
rca_steps:
1) Correlate tunnel flap timestamps with underlay latency/loss events
2) Check BFD timer settings vs underlay measured RTT — are timers too tight?
3) Check if NAT binding is changing (CGNAT) — tunnel source IP shifting?
4) Verify control connection to SD-WAN controller is stable
5) Check underlay keepalive packet loss specifically
6) Increase BFD hold timer or reduce detection multiplier as test
resolution: Tune BFD timers to tolerate underlay jitter; fix underlay instability; use static NAT instead of CGNAT; fix controller reachability.
preventive_action: Test BFD timer values with realistic underlay jitter before deployment; monitor tunnel stability KPI; alert on > 1 flap/hour.
related_tools: SD-WAN controller analytics, BFD logs, SNMP, underlay monitoring
priority: P2; P1 if flaps causing sustained application outages

faq: SD-WAN edge device cannot reach the SD-WAN controller — what is the impact and how to restore control plane?
likely_causes:
- Control link (MPLS/internet) from edge to controller down
- Controller cluster failure or maintenance
- Firewall blocking control plane ports (DTLS 12346, TLS 443)
- Certificate on edge expired — control plane TLS rejected
- DNS resolution failure for controller FQDN
- Edge device clock skew causing TLS certificate validation failure
observability_signals:
- controller_up == 0
- Control connection state: not active
- SD-WAN controller showing edge as disconnected
- Edge operating in 'local mode' — forwarding based on cached policy
- OMP sessions down on edge
cli_commands:
show sdwan control connections (Cisco vEdge/cEdge)
show sdwan system status
ping vrf <transport> <controller-ip>
traceroute vrf <transport> <controller-ip>
show certificate (check edge certificate validity)
nslookup <controller-fqdn>
show clock (NTP sync check)
rca_steps:
1) Test reachability to controller IP from edge transport VRF: ping and traceroute
2) Check if control ports are open: telnet/nc to controller on port 12346 or 443
3) Check edge certificate: is it expired?
4) Verify DNS resolves controller FQDN correctly
5) Check edge clock — large skew causes TLS certificate rejection
6) Check controller health from controller management console
resolution: Restore control link; open firewall ports; renew edge certificate; fix DNS; sync NTP; restore controller cluster.
preventive_action: Monitor control connection state; alert immediately on loss; certificate expiry monitoring; dual transport paths for control plane.
related_tools: SD-WAN controller management, NTP monitoring, certificate monitoring, SNMP
priority: P1 — policy changes cannot be pushed; edge running on stale config

faq: An entire branch site is unreachable via SD-WAN — how to determine root cause remotely?
likely_causes:
- Branch edge device powered off or crashed
- All WAN access circuits at branch failed (last-mile outage)
- Edge device NAT/IP address changed — tunnels cannot rebuild
- Power outage at branch site
- Edge device config corrupted after update
- ISP ceased service without notification
observability_signals:
- branch_reachable == 0 from SD-WAN controller
- All tunnels to branch site in down state
- Controller showing branch edge as disconnected
- No successful pings to any branch device
- Physical site contact reports power or connectivity issue
cli_commands:
show sdwan bfd sessions (from hub) — all sessions to branch
show sdwan omp peers | include <branch>
ping <branch-loopback-ip> from hub edge
check controller for last-seen time of branch edge
contact branch site out-of-band (phone, local contact)
check OOB management path if available
rca_steps:
1) Check controller: when was branch last seen? What was the last state?
2) From hub: are any tunnels to branch edge still up (partial access?)'
3) Check if all WAN circuits failed simultaneously — suggests power or LAN issue
4) Try OOB management path (LTE OOB, console server)
5) Contact local site: is power on? Is edge device LED status normal?
6) Check ISP circuit status for all branch circuits
resolution: Restore power; reboot edge device; restore internet/MPLS circuit; push replacement edge config if device failed.
preventive_action: Deploy OOB management (LTE-based) for all branches; monitor all tunnel states to each site; alert on complete site loss immediately.
related_tools: SD-WAN controller, OOB management, ISP portal, SNMP
priority: P1 — full site outage

faq: The RCA engine returned a low-confidence unknown result — what should the operator do?
likely_causes:
- Insufficient telemetry data to match any intent pattern
- New failure mode not covered by any existing intent
- Noisy or conflicting signals reducing all intent scores
- Single-metric event with no corroborating signals
- KB and intent coverage gap for this category/subcategory
- Event from unsupported device type or domain
observability_signals:
- combined_score < 0.6 across all intents
- No single intent score above 0.5
- Missing or null values for key metrics
- Event from device type not in topology/CMDB
- Multiple unrelated signals present simultaneously
cli_commands:
Review raw event payload in platform
Check device inventory: is this device in CMDB/topology?
Manually inspect device: show logging, show interface, show processes
Collect full diagnostics: show tech-support
Correlate with nearby device events in time window
rca_steps:
1) Review raw telemetry: what signals are present? Which are missing?
2) Check CMDB: is this device/resource registered and classified?
3) Gather full diagnostics from device or service
4) Look for similar events in adjacent time window on related devices
5) Escalate to SME for the device type/domain
6) After resolution: document root cause and create new KB/intent if novel failure
resolution: Manual investigation and escalation to domain SME; document findings; create new KB article and intent if novel failure type discovered.
preventive_action: Review unknown.low_confidence frequency; add coverage for top recurring unknown events; ensure all device types in CMDB; expand telemetry collection.
related_tools: Platform KB admin, CMDB, device CLI, escalation workflow
priority: P2 — investigate to determine real priority
